result:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about the moment a story decides it wants to be told. It’s almost never when I’m sitting at the keyboard, coffee beside me, fingers poised. It’s usually inconvenient: mid-shower, driving past a field at golden hour, halfway through a conversation I’m supposed to be listening to. A shard of something—overheard phrase, half-forgotten smell, the particular slant of light on a rusty shed—lodges under the skin and starts pulsing. For days it will throb quietly, demanding I acknowledge that it is alive, that it intends to grow lungs and legs and probably ruin a few nights’ sleep in the process.

If I ignore it, the ache migrates: into dreams, into the margins of meeting notes, into that liminal daze between wakefulness and alarm-clock panic. Eventually I capitulate, scribble the first paragraph on the back of a parking ticket or in the notes app while stopped at a red light, and suddenly the world dilates. Colors saturate; strangers’ gestures feel scripted for me; every song on the shuffle becomes an orchestrated overture. I’m not writing the story so much as holding the door while it shoves past me and strides into the room.

There’s a weird arrogance in this phase—an assumption that commuters on the train really do want to overhear me muttering dialogue, that the barista’s curious glance is actually research into a barista-character I might invent. But the opposite is happening too: the story is eavesdropping on me. It rifles through my childhood humiliations, plagiarizes ex-lovers’ voicemails, steals the exact cadence of my mother’s sigh when she’d find muddy footprints on the carpet. The more courage I muster to write, the more ruthlessly the story strips me for parts. It’s intimacy at knife-point.

Then, somewhere around page forty or fifty—long after the initial spark feels expendable—the whole enterprise tilts. The characters begin to answer back. I’ll type a sentence like “She storms out, slamming the door,” and instantly the protagonist refuses: No, I stay put; I say the thing I never said in real life. If I try to force my outline on them, prose turns wooden, claustrophobic. The book teaches me what it needs: looser chronology, a different point of view, a courage I haven’t yet earned. My job mutates from architect to custodian. I merely patrol the corridors, switch on lights, wipe graffiti from the walls so the next visitor can read the emergency exits clearly.

People sometimes ask, “How do you know when you’re finished?” The honest answer: you don’t. You simply reach a moment when the thought of re-entering that world feels like walking back into a party you left at 3 a.m.—music off, chairs upside-down, one sorry balloon drifting in a puddle of spilled beer. It’s not that the story’s perfect; it’s that it has outgrown your private neuroses and needs other minds to infect. So you hit “save” for the last time, export the file, and experience a bereavement so sneaky it masquerades as relief. For weeks you flinch from bookstores, scowl at subway ads for novels you once coveted. Language itself seems emptied of oxygen.

But here’s the part they never tell you: sometime later—months, years—someone else’s book, or film, or song, will detonate that same buried room inside you, and the whole cycle rekindles. Not because you found a new plot, but because you finally understood how completely a tale can own you, reshape your marrow, and release you back into daylight altered. And you realize that finished manuscript gathering dust under the bed wasn’t an ending; it was an inoculation—proof that you can survive the fever, emerge dizzy, absurdly grateful, and willing to catch it all over again, as soon as the next sharp, beautiful fragment plants itself under your skin and starts pulsing.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
